title: feat(release): allow diff remote overrides
summary: The PR correctly adds per-run `--remote` overrides for `release doctor` and `release pr preview`, with good test coverage for `parseAction`, `resolveDiffRemote`, `loadConfiguredPullRequestDiff`, and `buildPreviewDoctorSummary`. The main issues are: (1) a dead-abstraction wrapper `loadDiffRemote` that adds zero logic, and (2) the wiring in `runPrPreview` that connects `options.remote → diffRemote → explicitDiffRemote` is not integration-tested — only `buildPreviewDoctorSummary` is exercised directly, leaving the conditional spreading logic in `runPrPreview` uncovered.

findings.title: runPrPreview remote wiring is untested — only buildPreviewDoctorSummary is covered directly
findings.body:
The PR's test for runbook remote injection passes `explicitDiffRemote: 'fork'` directly to `buildPreviewDoctorSummary`, bypassing the logic in `runPrPreview` that computes and conditionally spreads it:

```typescript
// pr-preview.ts — untested wiring
const diffRemote = loadDiffRemote(config, options.remote)
...(options.remote ? { remote: options.remote } : {}),        // to diff loader
...(options.remote ? { explicitDiffRemote: diffRemote } : {}), // to summary
```

If someone changed the condition (e.g. switched from `options.remote` to a different check) or broke the `loadDiffRemote → explicitDiffRemote` chain, the existing tests would still pass. An integration test calling `runPrPreview({ remote: 'fork' })` with mocked services and asserting the resulting runbook commands would close this gap.

findings.title: `loadDiffRemote` is a dead-abstraction wrapper with no added logic
Raw vs normalized remote: two divergent normalization paths in runPrPreview
findings.body:
In `runPrPreview`, `diffRemote` is normalized via `loadDiffRemote` (trim + empty-check) and used for the runbook display. But `loadConfiguredPullRequestDiff` receives the raw `options.remote`, which normalizes independently inside `resolveDiffRemote`. They produce the same result today, but the code suggests two separate sources of truth for the effective remote:

```typescript
const diffRemote = loadDiffRemote(config, options.remote)          // normalized
yield* loadConfiguredPullRequestDiff({ ..., remote: options.remote }) // also normalizes
```

Passing `diffRemote` to `loadConfiguredPullRequestDiff` instead of `options.remote` would make the actual remote used for diffs and the one shown in the runbook share a single normalization point.

residual_risks: Remote names accepted from the CLI are not validated against git's allowed remote name format. A value like `fork/../secret` would be passed into git args and could produce unexpected git behavior, though not a shell-injection risk since git is spawned directly (not via a shell). Consider a simple regex guard on remote names at the parse layer.

Doctor command's `remote r` parameter uses `Schema.UndefinedOr(Schema.String)` which accepts whitespace-only strings. These silently fall through to `origin` after `normalizeRemoteOverride` trims them, which may confuse operators who pass `--remote '   '` expecting an error.
